mcqs.explanation: The outer loop runs n times, and for each i, the inner loop runs i times. The total iterations = 1 + 2 + 3 + ... + n = n(n+1)/2 = Θ(n²).

mcqs.question: Consider the recurrence relation: T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n, with T(1) = O(1). Using the Master Theorem, what is the asymptotic complexity?
mcqs.explanation: Here a=2, b=2, f(n)=n. Since n^logb(a) = n^1 = n, we have f(n) = Θ(n^logb(a)), which is Case 2 of Master Theorem, giving T(n) = Θ(n log n).

mcqs.question: For QuickSort, what is the time complexity for best case, average case, and worst case respectively?

options.text: O(n log n), O(n log n), O(n²)
O(n²), O(n log n), O(n log n)

mcqs.explanation: QuickSort has O(n log n) in best and average cases (when pivot divides array evenly), but degrades to O(n²) when the pivot is always the smallest or largest element.

mcqs.question: Which of the following statements is FALSE about Big O, Big Ω, and Big Θ notations?

options.text: If f(n) = O(g(n)), then g(n) = Ω(f(n))
If f(n) = Θ(g(n)), then f(n) = O(g(n)) and f(n) = Ω(g(n))
Big O notation always gives an upper bound that is asymptotically tight

mcqs.explanation: Big O notation provides an upper bound, but it is not always tight. For example, n = O(n²) is true but not tight. Big Θ provides a tight bound.

mcqs.question: What is the time complexity of the following recurrence: T(n) = T(n-1) + c, where c is a constant?
mcqs.explanation: Unrolling the recurrence: T(n) = T(n-1) + c = T(n-2) + 2c = ... = T(1) + (n-1)c = O(n). This represents linear time.
The algorithm makes exactly one pass through the array, performing constant-time operations at each iteration, resulting in Θ(n) time complexity.

mcqs.question: Which of the following functions has the highest growth rate as n approaches infinity?
mcqs.explanation: 2^n is exponential, which grows faster than any polynomial (n^k) or polylogarithmic function. Note: 3^(log₂(n)) = n^(log₂(3)) ≈ n^1.585, a polynomial.

mcqs.question: A binary search tree (BST) has n nodes. What is the worst-case time complexity to search for an element, where the tree can be unbalanced?
mcqs.explanation: In the worst case (skewed BST like a linked list), the search may traverse all n nodes, giving O(n) time complexity. Balanced BST gives O(log n).
